title: Native DuckDB vs Direct Parquet Querying Performance
text:
Setup


Show code
library(arrow)
library(duckdb)
library(DBI)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(here)
library(RMySQL)

title: Native DuckDB vs Direct Parquet Querying Performance
text:
Step 1: Build Storage Formats
This step can be done in many approaches. For this guide, a connection with MySQL is setup, then three tables from the entire SQL database are used to write to its own Parquet file. Then a native DuckDB database is built with each city (Parquet file) as its own table. This only needs to run once so set eval: false after the databases are built.


Show code
Sys.setenv(MARIADB_TLS_DISABLE_PEER_VERIFICATION = 1)

con_traffic <- DBI::dbConnect(
  RMySQL::MySQL(),
  dbname   = "traffic",
  host     = Sys.getenv("TRAFFIC_HOST"),
  user     = Sys.getenv("TRAFFIC_USER"),
  password = Sys.getenv("TRAFFIC_PWD")
)

tables     <- c("ca_los_angeles_2020_04_01",
                "ca_san_francisco_2020_04_01",
                "ca_san_jose_2020_04_01")
short_names <- c("la", "sf", "sj")

# ── Write one Parquet file per city ──
dir.create(here::here("parquet_cities"), showWarnings = FALSE)

for (i in seq_along(tables)) {
  cat("Reading", tables[i], "from MySQL...\n")
  df      <- dbReadTable(con_traffic, tables[i])
  outfile <- here::here("parquet_cities", paste0(short_names[i], ".parquet"))
  write_parquet(df, outfile)
  cat("  Written:", outfile, "\n")
  rm(df); gc()
}

# ── Build native DuckDB — one table per city ──
con_native <- dbConnect(duckdb(), here::here("traffic_native.duckdb"))

for (i in seq_along(tables)) {
  cat("Loading", tables[i], "into DuckDB...\n")
  df <- dbReadTable(con_traffic, tables[i])
  dbWriteTable(con_native, short_names[i], df, overwrite = TRUE)
  cat("  Rows written:", nrow(df), "\n")
  rm(df); gc()
}

cat("\nTables in native DuckDB:\n")
print(dbListTables(con_native))

dbDisconnect(con_native, shutdown = TRUE)
dbDisconnect(con_traffic)
cat("Done.\n")

title: Native DuckDB vs Direct Parquet Querying Performance
text:
Step 2: Storage Size Comparison
One concrete advantage of direct Parquet over native DuckDB is storage footprint and size. Because Parquet files are written with columnar compression applied at the column level, they are typically substantially smaller than an equivalent DuckDB database, which stores data in its own internal format optimized for query performance rather than disk compactness.


Show code
parquet_files <- list.files(here::here("parquet_cities"),
                             pattern = "\\.parquet$", full.names = TRUE)
parquet_size_mb <- round(sum(file.size(parquet_files)) / 1e6, 2)
parquet_size_gb <- round(parquet_size_mb / 1000, 3)

duckdb_size_mb <- round(file.size(here::here("traffic_native.duckdb")) / 1e6, 2)
duckdb_size_gb <- round(duckdb_size_mb / 1000, 3)

ratio <- round(duckdb_size_mb / parquet_size_mb, 2)

size_df <- data.frame(
  Format        = c("Direct Parquet (3 files)", "Native DuckDB (1 file)"),
  `Size (MB)`   = c(parquet_size_mb, duckdb_size_mb),
  `Size (GB)`   = c(parquet_size_gb, duckdb_size_gb),
  `Files`       = c(3, 1),
  `Ratio vs Parquet` = c(1.00, ratio)
)

knitr::kable(size_df, align = "r",
  caption = "Storage size comparison: direct Parquet vs native DuckDB")



Storage size comparison: direct Parquet vs native DuckDB









Format
Size..MB.
Size..GB.
Files
Ratio.vs.Parquet




Direct Parquet (3 files)
90.24
0.090
3
1.00


Native DuckDB (1 file)
312.49
0.312
1
3.46





Beyond storage size, direct Parquet also avoids the ingestion step entirely. The data can be queried immediately after the Parquet files are written, with no loading or database construction required. The trade-off is query performance, which the remainder of this page explores.

title: Native DuckDB vs Direct Parquet Querying Performance
text:
Step 3: Benchmark
This section contains the code for creating the queries and obtaining the mean times for each query. In this code, 5 queries were performed and are meant to represent varying forms of queries, from counting rows to cross-table queries.


Show code
la_parquet <- here::here("parquet_cities", "la.parquet")
sf_parquet <- here::here("parquet_cities", "sf.parquet")
sj_parquet <- here::here("parquet_cities", "sj.parquet")

queries <- list(

  Q1 = list(
    label  = "COUNT(*) — LA only",
    direct = sprintf("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM read_parquet('%s')", la_parquet),
    native = "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM la"
  ),

  Q2 = list(
    label  = "GROUP BY subject_race — LA only",
    direct = sprintf(
      "SELECT subject_race, COUNT(*) AS n FROM read_parquet('%s') GROUP BY subject_race ORDER BY n DESC",
      la_parquet),
    native = "SELECT subject_race, COUNT(*) AS n FROM la GROUP BY subject_race ORDER BY n DESC"
  ),

  Q3 = list(
    label  = "GROUP BY type — LA only",
    direct = sprintf(
      "SELECT type, COUNT(*) AS n FROM read_parquet('%s') GROUP BY type ORDER BY n DESC",
      la_parquet),
    native = "SELECT type, COUNT(*) AS n FROM la GROUP BY type ORDER BY n DESC"
  ),

  Q4 = list(
    label  = "String filter ILIKE — LA only",
    direct = sprintf(
      "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM read_parquet('%s') WHERE raw_descent_description ILIKE '%%hispanic%%'",
      la_parquet),
    native = "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM la WHERE raw_descent_description ILIKE '%hispanic%'"
  ),

  Q5 = list(
    label  = "Cross-table GROUP BY subject_race — all three cities",
    direct = sprintf(
      "SELECT subject_race, COUNT(*) AS n FROM (
         SELECT subject_race FROM read_parquet('%s')
         UNION ALL
         SELECT subject_race FROM read_parquet('%s')
         UNION ALL
         SELECT subject_race FROM read_parquet('%s')
       ) GROUP BY subject_race ORDER BY n DESC",
      la_parquet, sf_parquet, sj_parquet),
    native = "SELECT subject_race, COUNT(*) AS n FROM (
         SELECT subject_race FROM la
         UNION ALL
         SELECT subject_race FROM sf
         UNION ALL
         SELECT subject_race FROM sj
       ) GROUP BY subject_race ORDER BY n DESC"
  )
)




Show code
fresh_direct <- function() dbConnect(duckdb())
fresh_native <- function() dbConnect(duckdb(), here::here("traffic_native.duckdb"))

n_warmup <- 3
n_trials <- 20

run_trials <- function(con_fn, sql, n_warmup, n_trials) {
  for (i in seq_len(n_warmup)) {
    con <- con_fn()
    try(dbGetQuery(con, sql), silent = TRUE)
    try(dbDisconnect(con, shutdown = TRUE), silent = TRUE)
    gc()
  }
  times_ms <- numeric(n_trials)
  for (i in seq_len(n_trials)) {
    con <- con_fn()
    tryCatch({
      times_ms[i] <- system.time(dbGetQuery(con, sql))["elapsed"] * 1000
    }, error = function(e) {
      times_ms[i] <<- NA
    }, finally = {
      try(dbDisconnect(con, shutdown = TRUE), silent = TRUE)
    })
    gc()
  }
  times_ms
}

all_trials <- data.frame(
  query_id   = character(),
  label      = character(),
  format     = character(),
  trial      = integer(),
  time_ms    = numeric()
)

for (qid in names(queries)) {
  q <- queries[[qid]]
  cat("\nRunning", qid, "—", q$label, "\n")

  cat("  Native...\n")
  nt <- run_trials(fresh_native, q$native, n_warmup, n_trials)
  cat("  Native mean:", round(mean(nt, na.rm = TRUE), 1), "ms\n")

  cat("  Direct...\n")
  dt <- run_trials(fresh_direct, q$direct, n_warmup, n_trials)
  cat("  Direct mean:", round(mean(dt, na.rm = TRUE), 1), "ms\n")

  all_trials <- rbind(all_trials,
    data.frame(query_id = qid, label = q$label, format = "native",
               trial = seq_len(n_trials), time_ms = round(nt, 3)),
    data.frame(query_id = qid, label = q$label, format = "direct",
               trial = seq_len(n_trials), time_ms = round(dt, 3))
  )
}

title: Native DuckDB vs Direct Parquet Querying Performance
text:
Conclusion
The results show a distinguishable difference that native DuckDB offers faster and more consistent query performance across most or all of the query types. Direct Parquet offers the advantage that files can be queried immediately after writing and occupy significantly less disk space.
The results can be understood based on the knowledge of how DuckDB querying and direct Parquet querying functions. When data is ingested into a native DuckDB database, DuckDB builds and maintains its own internal columnar storage format, statistics, and metadata that are optimized specifically for its query engine. When a query runs against a native table, DuckDB can exploit these internals directly, for example, reading only the pages it needs, using pre-computed column statistics, and operating entirely within a single file with no coordination overhead. Direct Parquet querying, by contrast, requires DuckDB to open each file, parse its footer, decompress column chunks, and assemble results from separate sources on every query execution. Even though Parquet is also columnar and compressed, this per-query parsing and file-handling overhead adds time that the native format avoids entirely.
For workflows involving repeated analytical queries over stable data, native DuckDB is the stronger choice. For workflows where storage efficiency, portability, or avoiding ingestion latency matters more than raw query speed, direct Parquet is competitive and in some cases faster.

title: SQL, Keys, Indexes, and B+ Trees
text:
● SQL Database Essentials
    SQL, Keys,Indexes &B+ Trees
    What is SQL? What are its primary features? How does it Index? How does a tree structures power billions of queries per second.
    
      SQL Foundations
      Keys & Constraints
      Index Structures
      B+ Tree Internals
    
    Scroll to explore↓
  




  01
  
    Foundations
    What is SQL & how do tables work?
    SQL is the language of relational databases. Instead of storing everything in one spreadsheet or file, SQL stores related pieces of information in separate tables. Queries combine those tables only when needed.

        Defining keys in SQL
        
CREATE TABLE students (
  student_id  INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name        VARCHAR(100),
  major      VARCHAR(100)
);
CREATE TABLE enrollments (
  enrollment_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  student_id    INT,
  course_id    INT,
  FOREIGN KEY (student_id)
    REFERENCES students(student_id)
);
-- Add a primary key after creation:
ALTER TABLE students
ADD PRIMARY KEY (student_id);
-- Add a foreign key after creation:
ALTER TABLE enrollments
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_student
FOREIGN KEY (student_id)
REFERENCES students(student_id);
        
        
          
            PRIMARY KEY
            Defined at table creation or added later with ALTER TABLE
          
          
            FOREIGN KEY
            Defined at table creation or added later with ADD CONSTRAINT
          
        
      
    

  

  
  

    
      1.1 · SQL Foundations
      SQL is declarative and remote
      SQL (Structured Query Language) lets you create, query, update, and manage relational databases. A relational database stores data in tables — rows are records, columns are attributes/variables.
      The key insight: SQL is declarative. You specify what data you want; the database engine's optimizer figures out how to retrieve it efficiently. This is why the same SQL query can run substantially faster with an index than without.
      SQL queries are sent to a server (SQL server) where the database is stored, though this server is often remote, that doesn't have to be the case. SQL handles everything in executing the query and returns the resulting data to the user.
      On the left is an example of a SQL query on the table students that retreives the name and major of Sophmore students in the table.
      
        Declarative
        Relational
        Structured
        Query Language
      
    

    
      1.2 · Tables & Relationships
      Relational aspect of SQL and Keys
      A table represents an entity — students, courses, orders. Each row is one record; each column is one attribute. A primary key uniquely identifies each row.
      A primary key must be unique and never NULL. Most tables have one primary key, though composite keys (multiple columns) are valid and identify rows through a composition of multiple columns.
      Relational databases become powerful when multiple tables are connected. To do this: A foreign key is a column that references another table's primary key. A foreign key doesn't have to be made up of unique values but it does need to reference a primary key (which is made up of unique values). It enforces referential integrity — you cannot enroll a student who doesn't exist. The database prevents the inconsistency at the constraint level.
      The student_id column bridges the students and enrollments tables which enables powerful joins without redundancy. A foreign key defines and enforces a relationship between tables.
      
        Entities
        Attributes
        No Duplication
        Joins
      
    

    
      1.3 · Primary & Foreign Keys
      How to create keys in context
      On the left is the SQL command for creating primary and foreign keys in a SQL database.
      The CREATE TABLE command is used when one creates the key at the same time as creating the SQL table itself.
      The ALTER TABLE command is used when the table was already created but has no pre-defined key. For a primary key within the query one would use ADD PRIMARY KEY and for a foreign key within the query one would use ADD CONSTRAINT and must specify the constraint is a foreign key and the primary key that the foreign key references.
      Common confusion: a foreign key is simply a column in your table. The relationship is established from by declaring it as a reference to another table's primary key with a FOREIGN KEY constraint.
      
        Unique
        Not NULL
        Referential Integrity
        Composite Keys
      
    

  




  
    Key insight
    Keys define relationships. Indexes make those relationships fast to query.
    A primary key is a logical constraint (a column). An index is the underlying data structure the database mainatains to turn a key lookup from O(n) into O(log n). They coexist but are fundamentally different things.
  




  02
  
    Performance Structures
    Indexes
    An index is a separate ordered data structure that helps the database find rows faster by storing indexed column values and references to the corresponding table rows.
  





  

    
    
      
        Physical rows vs. Index order
        
          
            Physical table
            Row 1 Tom
            Row 2 Alex
            Row 4 Nina
            Row 7 Maria
            Row 9 Sam
          
          →
          
            Index on name
            Alex → Row 2
            Maria → Row 7
            Nina → Row 4
            Sam → Row 9
            Tom → Row 1
          
        
        
CREATE INDEX idx_name
  ON students(name);
CREATE INDEX idx_enroll_student
  ON enrollments(student_id);
        
      
    

    
    
      
        ❌ Full table scan (no index)
        
        Every row checked. Complexity: O(n).
        ✅ Index lookup
        
          Root
          →
          Branch
          →
          Leaf
          →
          Match!
        
        
          
            O(n)
            Full scan
          
          
            O(log n)
            Index lookup
          
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Index tradeoffs
        
          
            ✅ Reads
            Faster WHEREFaster JOINsEfficient ORDER BYFast range queries
          
          
            ⚠️ Writes
            Slower INSERTSlower UPDATESlower DELETEExtra storage
          
        
        
        Selectivity matters
        
          
            email = 'x@example.com'High ✓
            
            Matches ~1 row of millions
          
          
            status = 'active'Low ✗
            
            Matches 60% of rows — index barely helps
          
          
            country = 'US'Medium
            
            Depends on data distribution
          
        
      
    

  

  

    
      2.1 · What is an Index
      An index is a sorted, separate access structure
      An index is a separate data structure — like a phonebook or a textbook's back index — that helps the database find rows without reading every single one.
      The physical rows in a table can be in any order. An index stores keys in sorted logical order with references (row pointers) to where those rows actually live. The table itself doesn't move.
      Important: a key is a logical constraint (unique, not null). An index is a physical performance structure. Primary keys usually get an index automatically in MySQL/InnoDB — but the concepts are distinct.
      
        Sorted Keys
        Row References
        Separate Structure
        Faster Lookup
      
    

    
      2.2 · Why Indexes Help
      Advantage: Prevents scanning every row, by following a guided path
      Without an index on email, finding WHERE email = 'x@example.com' requires inspecting every row — a full table scan. With millions of rows this leads to computing inefficiencies and prolonged runtimes.
      With an index, the database follows an ordered tree path — root → branch → leaf — touching only a handful of nodes to land on the match. Essentially, an index cuts down the total number of comparisons needed to identify a row. This is the difference between O(n), which is the runtime without an index, and O(log n) which is the decreased runtime through an index.
      Indexes are most impactful for WHERE filters, JOIN conditions, ORDER BY sorting, and range queries like BETWEEN. Below the structure and functionality of an index is explained in depth.
      
        WHERE clauses
        JOIN conditions
        ORDER BY
        Range queries
      
    

    
      2.3 · Limitations & Tradeoffs
      Indexes not always fast and beneficial
      Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes. Every INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE must also update every relevant index which causes the structure of the index to alter. Too many indexes increase maintenance cost and storage of the database.
      Selectivity is critical. An index on a column with few distinct values (like a boolean is_active or status) may not help — too many rows match, and the database may choose a full scan anyway since that may be more efficient.
       Although primary keys must be unique by definition, a regular index (created with CREATE INDEX) has no uniqueness requirement at all. Sometimes, regular indexes can be helpful to speed up row retrieval and querying even if they aren't for primary keys. It is still important to consider selectivity or uniqueness when creating a regular index, as an index on a column with low selectivity may not provide performance benefits and can even degrade performance due to the overhead of maintaining the index and the potential for increased table lookups.
        
      Even with a perfect index, a query can still be slow if it matches many rows (leaf scanning) or those rows are scattered across many storage pages and has to perform many table lookups. Table lookups happen when the database engine jumps from the index back to the main table to retrieve a full row of data after finding a match in the index. This is not a problem in MySQL because the table data lives directly in the primary key's B+ tree leaves (clustered index), so there's no second trip. Secondary indexes, however, (on non-primary-key columns) always require a second lookup back to the primary key tree to get the full row.
      
        Write overhead
        Low selectivity
        Leaf scanning
        Table lookups
      
    

  




  
    So what structure does an index use?
    Most database indexes are B trees. MySQL uses B+ trees which are a balanced tree structure designed for disk-based storage with high fanout and sorted leaf chains.
    Understanding the tree explains why indexes are fast for both equality lookups and range queries, and why they stay efficient even with millions of rows.
  




  03
  
    Data Structures
    B+ Trees
    The tree structure that makes indexes fast and optimized in MySQL.
  





  

    
    
      
        
          B+ Tree Structure
        
        
          
        
        
          Root node (top) → branch nodes (middle) → leaf nodes (bottom, linked in sorted order). Photo credit: (Winand, 2026)
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Search path for key 57
        
          
            
              
                
              
              
                
              
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            

            
            39 | 83 | 98
            
            < 39
            46|53|57|83
            83-98
            
            42|48|51
            55 | 57 | 57
            61|72|79
            
            
            KEY 57 FOUND ✓
          
        
        
          1Root [39|83|98]: 57 > 39 and < 83 → middle pointer
          2Branch [46|53|57|83]: 57 = 57 → leaf pointer
          3Leaf [55|57|57]: key found, return row reference
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Why B+ trees stay shallow
        
          Fanout of 100 → 3 levels covers
          1,000,000
          leaf entries with only 3 page reads
        
        
        
          
            ROOT
          
          ↓ × many pointers
          
            Br
            Br
            Br
            ...
          
          ↓ × many pointers
          
            Lf
            Lf
            Lf
            Lf
            ...
          
        
        
          
            Equality
            Root → leaf in O(log n)
          
          
            Range
            Walk sorted leaf chain
          
        
      
    

  

  

    
      3.1 · B+ Tree Structure
      Root, branches, leaves
      A B+ tree has three node types. The root at the top is the entry point for every search. Branch nodes in the middle store only keys and pointers — they guide the search but hold no row data. Leaf nodes at the bottom store the actual index entries and row references.
      In MySQL/InnoDB, the primary key index is clustered — the actual table row data lives in the leaf nodes of the primary key B+ tree. Secondary indexes store the indexed column value plus the corresponding primary key, requiring a second tree lookup to retrieve the full row.
      
        Root node
        Branch nodes
        Leaf nodes
        Clustered index
      
    

    
      3.2 · Leaf Chain
      Leaf nodes are linked for range scans
      One of the key advantages of B+ trees over regular binary trees: leaf nodes are linked left-to-right in sorted order. This dashed green chain in the diagram is what enables efficient range queries.
      To answer WHERE score BETWEEN 43 AND 67, the database traverses the tree to find 43, then simply walks the leaf chain to collect everything up to 67 — no re-traversal needed.
      
        Sorted chain
        Range scans
        BETWEEN
        ORDER BY
      
    

    
      3.3 · Tree Traversal
      Tree traversal walk-through
      Consider the task where the goal is to search for a key 57: start at the root [39|83|98]. Since 57 is between 39 and 83, follow the middle pointer. At branch [46|53|57|83], find the position of 57 and follow to the leaf [55|57|57]. Found.
      Notice that only one node is visited per level. This is always true for any key lookup in a B+ tree, regardless of the key or the size of the data. At each node, the search key is compared against the stored keys and exactly one pointer is followed downward. The result is that the number of nodes inspected is always exactly equal to the depth of the tree. This is where the O(log n) runtime comes from: each level cuts the search space by the branching factor, and the tree's depth grows only logarithmically with the number of rows.
      
        O(log n)
        One path
        Exact lookup
      
    

    
      3.4 · Efficiency & Fanout
      Shallow trees handle millions of rows in 3–4 reads
      Each node in a B+ tree corresponds to one page (typically 16 KB in MySQL/InnoDB). Because a page can hold hundreds of keys, the fanout — the number of entries per node — is very high.
      With fanout 100: level 1 (root) covers 100 branches. Level 2 covers 10,000. Level 3 covers 1,000,000 leaves. Therefore, a tree covering a million rows reads only 3 pages for any lookup. That's why B+ trees scale so well.
      The tree is always balanced — all leaf nodes sit at the same depth — because insertions and deletions trigger automatic node splits and merges that propagate up the tree to maintain this property. This guarantees predictable search time regardless of which key you look up.
      
        Efficient
        Always balanced
        Predictable
      
    


 
      3.4 · Efficiency & Fanout
      Index structures differ across SQL dialects
      MySQL primarily uses B+ trees to structure its indexes, but other dialects treat indexing slightly differently. For example, PostgreSQL and SQLite default to B tree indexing, where (among other differences) row data is stored in all levels of the tree rather than just in the leaves. In Postgres, it is also possible to manually choose alternative indexing strategies like hash (simple and efficient for equality comparisons) or GiST operating classes (for geometric data and nearest-neighbor queries). Generally, though, understanding the structure of B and B+ trees is essential to creating and working with indexes in SQL.  
      
        B vs. B+ trees
        Manual index structure selection
      
    

  




  04
  
    Best Practices
    Practical indexing: What to take away from indexing
    Knowing the theory behind indexes can be helpful but only if you know how to utilize the information for your own good.
  





  

    
    
      
        Composite index: (customer_id, order_date)
        
          Sorted by customer_id first, then order_date within each customer:
          cust: 12024-01-05
          cust: 12024-03-12
          cust: 12024-07-01
          cust: 22024-02-18
          cust: 22024-09-30
          · · ·

          
            cust: 10
            2024-01-09 ← found
          
        
        
CREATE INDEX idx_cust_date
  ON orders(customer_id, order_date);
EXPLAIN
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 10
ORDER BY order_date;
        
      
    

    
    
      

        
        
          SQL indexes: OLTP vs OLAP
        

        
        

          
          
            
              ✅ OLTP
            
            Transactional
            
              Finds few specific rows
              B+ tree skips most data
              Joins on foreign keys
              WHERE, lookup, update
            
            O(log n) and index beneficial
          

          
          
            
              ⚠️ OLAP
            
            Analytical
            
              Scans many/all rows
              Index can't help a full scan
              Aggregates whole columns
              AVG, SUM, GROUP BY
            
            O(n) and index not beneficial
          

        

        
        
        
          
            Use SQL + B+ tree indexes for transactional row lookups.
            Use DuckDB / Snowflake for analytical column scans.
          
        

      
    

  

  

    
      4.1 · Composite Indexes
      Column order (during creation) in composite indexes is critical
      A composite index on (customer_id, order_date) sorts first by customer_id, then by order_date within each customer group. This makes queries that filter by customer_id alone very efficient.
      It also helps queries filtering by both columns, and sorting by order_date after filtering by customer_id. However, a query filtering only by order_date won't benefit much — the index isn't sorted by that column alone.
      What to take away: put the column you filter on most frequently first when creating a composite index. Doing so improves both lookup speed and query efficiency compared to any other column ordering.
      
        Leading column first
        Prefix matching
        Column order matters
      
    

    
      4.2 · Guidelines
      Indexes make SQL fast for row-based, transactional queries
      SQL indexes are purpose-built for OLTP — Online Transactional Processing. These are queries that locate and return a small number of specific rows: find one customer, update one order, look up one student. The B+ tree traverses from root to leaf in O(log n) and lands on exactly the rows you need, touching almost nothing else in the table.
      This is fundamentally different from analytical queries — the kind that ask "what is the average grade across all students?" or "how many orders were placed per month this year?" Those queries need to scan many or all rows and aggregate across entire columns. An index does not help here because there is no shortcut — every row must be read regardless. This workload is called OLAP (Online Analytical Processing).
      SQL finds the right rows fast through its indexes, but analytical queries need to read one column across millions of rows meaning the database still has to touch every row's storage page. This makes analytical queries less efficient in SQL compared to transactional queries (row-based). However, based on the index model of SQL, SQL is especially efficient and strong for row-based queries.
      The practical takeaway: use SQL and its B+ tree indexes for transactional workloads like precise lookups, joins on foreign keys, filtering by specific values. For large-scale analytical work across many rows and few columns, a column-oriented system is the better architectural choice (like DuckDB or Snowflake).
      
        OLTP
        Row lookup
        OLAP
        Column scan
      
    

  




  Key Takeaways
  
    
      01
      SQL is a declarative language, a query is sent to a remote SQL server, and the optimizer decides how to retrieve it efficiently. Rows are records, columns are attributes.
    
    
      02
      Primary keys uniquely identify rows and must be unique and never NULL. Foreign keys are columns that reference another table's primary key. They create relationships and enforce referential integrity. Both can be defined at table creation or added later with ALTER TABLE.
    
    
      03
      Indexes are separate physical structures, distinct from keys, that store column values in sorted order with row references. They reduce lookups from O(n) full scans to O(log n) guided tree traversal. Composite indexes require putting the most-filtered column first for maximum efficiency.
    
    
      04
      B+ trees power MySQL indexes. The root is the entry point, branch nodes guide the search, and leaf nodes store the data. Leaf nodes are linked in sorted order enabling fast range scans. With high fanout, just 3–4 levels can cover millions of rows.
    
    
      05
      Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes — every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE must also update the index structure. Low-selectivity columns (like booleans or status fields) benefit little from indexing since too many rows match.
    
    
      06
      SQL indexes excel at OLTP, precise row lookups, joins on foreign keys, transactional queries. For OLAP analytical workloads that scan entire columns across millions of rows, column-oriented systems like DuckDB or Snowflake are the better architectural choice.

title: SQL Upload Guide
text:
If you do statistics or data science, your default instinct when handed a pile of CSVs is probably to load each one into R or Python and start joining data frames in memory. Working with separate files, however, may lead to issues including files that are too big to fit comfortably in RAM or re-running the same read_csv() calls every session.
A relational database solves these problems by moving the structure of your data out of your analysis script and into the data itself. Instead of a folder of CSVs that only mean something in light of whatever script last touched them, you get a single, queryable system where the relationships between tables, the rules about what counts as valid data, and the logic for combining tables all live in one place.
Concretely, that gets you:
Speed and memory efficiency at scale. A database engine reads only the columns and rows a query actually needs, rather than loading entire files into memory first.
A single source of truth. Once data lives in a database, every person and every script that connects to it sees the same version.
Enforced data integrity. A database can refuse to let you insert nonsense: a row that references an actor who doesn’t exist, a duplicate ID where uniqueness is required. As described below, keys are the mechanism that makes the enforcement possible, and they’re worth understanding even if you are not the person creating the SQL database.
Reusable, composable queries. SQL’s JOIN machinery lets you combine tables on demand, rather than writing bespoke merge logic every time you need a different combination of variables.
We’ll walk through all of this concretely, using a real dataset: every cast member, host, sketch, and impression from Saturday Night Live, scraped from a public repository and loaded into a MariaDB server.

title: SQL Upload Guide
section: Part 1: Getting Data Into a Database
text:
Part 1: Getting Data Into a Database

Where the Data Comes From
For this guide, the source data comes from the snldb GitHub repository (Hilleckes and Morris 2017), which contains a Python scraper for SNL cast, episode, sketch, and appearance data. The specifics of this scraper don’t matter much beyond getting the data necessary to walk through the ideas below. What matters is the general pattern: most real-world data starts out as scraped, exported, or otherwise messy flat files, and getting it into a proper database is itself a meaningful step.
pip install -r requirements.txt
./crawl_single_episode.sh   # test run: one episode
./crawl_all.sh               # full crawl: every episode
python convert_json_to_csv.py
After running the Python code, you end up with a folder of .csv files, one per entity type (e.g., actors, episodes, sketches, etc.). A set of flat files is the starting point that most statisticians actually encounter: a pile of CSVs, related to each other in ways that exist only in your mind (or in a README) but not yet enforced anywhere


Connecting R to a SQL Server
The general pattern for connecting to any SQL database from R is the same regardless of which database engine (e.g., MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, SQL Server) sits on the other end: you load a driver package, supply connection credentials, and get back a connection object that every subsequent query uses.


Show the code
library(DBI)
library(RMariaDB)
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(purrr)
library(janitor)


A practice worth adopting immediately, regardless of what data you’re working with: never write credentials directly into a script. Store them as environment variables in a .Renviron file (which should never be committed to version control), and read them in using Sys.getenv() (Wickham and Bryan 2023).


Show the code
SQL_USER <- Sys.getenv("SQL_USER")
SQL_PWD  <- Sys.getenv("SQL_PWD")
SQL_HOST <- Sys.getenv("SQL_HOST")
SQL_DB   <- "snl"




Show the code
Sys.setenv(MySQL_TLS_DISABLE_PEER_VERIFICATION = 1)

con_snl <- DBI::dbConnect(
  RMySQL::MySQL(),
  host = SQL_HOST,
  user = SQL_USER,
  password = SQL_PWD,
  dbname = SQL_DB,
  load_data_local_infile = FALSE
)

DBI::dbListTables(con_snl)


dbListTables() is worth calling immediately after establishing a connection, regardless of context. It’s the fastest way to confirm the connection actually succeeded and to see what already exists on the server before you touch anything.


Locating and Validating Source Files Before Upload
Before uploading anything, it’s worth building the habit of checking that your source files are what you think they are. This step generalizes far beyond SNL data: any time you’re about to bulk-load files into a database, a quick sanity pass catches problems while they’re still cheap to fix.


Show the code
csv_folder <- "~/Desktop/Summer 2026/snldb/output"

csv_files <- list.files(
  path = csv_folder,
  pattern = "\\.csv$",
  full.names = TRUE
)

csv_files
length(csv_files)

if (length(csv_files) == 0) {
  stop("No CSV files found. Check csv_folder path.")
}




Show the code
csv_check <- map_dfr(csv_files, function(file_path) {
  df <- read_csv(file_path, show_col_types = FALSE)
  
  tibble(
    file = basename(file_path),
    rows = nrow(df),
    columns = ncol(df)
  )
})

csv_check


# A tibble: 11 × 3
   file             rows columns
   <chr>           <int>   <int>
 1 actors.csv       2306       4
 2 appearances.csv 55355       9
 3 casts.csv         614       8
 4 characters.csv   1099       3
 5 episodes.csv      906       4
 6 hosts.csv         925       2
 7 impressions.csv  4115       3
 8 seasons.csv        46       5
 9 sketches.csv      301       2
10 tenure.csv        154       4
11 titles.csv      13704       7


The script above produces a small summary table — file name, row count, column count — for every file about to be uploaded. It seems like a small thing, but it’s the cheapest possible defense against uploading an empty file, a truncated file, or a file you grabbed from the wrong folder.


From File Names to Table Names
SQL table names have stricter rules than file names: no spaces, no leading numbers, no arbitrary punctuation. Rather than handling an ad hoc for every file, it’s worth writing a small helper function that any future project can reuse:


Show the code
clean_table_name <- function(file_path) {
  file_path |>
    basename() |>
    str_remove("\\.csv$") |>
    janitor::make_clean_names()
}


janitor::make_clean_names() does the heavy lifting — lowercasing, replacing spaces and punctuation with underscores, and resolving duplicate names — which is exactly the kind of small, boring utility worth keeping in your personal toolkit across projects.


Uploading in Chunks, Not All at Once
There is a temptation, with a clean CSV, to dump the whole thing into a database using a single INSERT statement. In practice, a single dump is often slower and less stable than chunking the upload — especially over a network connection to a remote server, where one enormous statement can often time out or simply get rejected outright. Therefore, the safest way to create a database is to upload data in chunks.


Show the code
upload_csv_to_sql <- function(file_path) {
  table_name <- clean_table_name(file_path)
  
  message("Reading ", basename(file_path), "...")
  
  df <- readr::read_csv(file_path, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
    janitor::clean_names()
  
  message("Rows in CSV: ", nrow(df))
  message("Columns in CSV: ", ncol(df))
  
  if (nrow(df) == 0) {
    stop("CSV has 0 rows: ", basename(file_path))
  }
  
  message("Uploading as table `", table_name, "`...")
  
  DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, paste0("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `", table_name, "`;"))
  
  DBI::dbCreateTable(
    conn = con_snl,
    name = table_name,
    fields = df
  )
  
  chunk_size <- 250
  n <- nrow(df)
  starts <- seq(1, n, by = chunk_size)
  
  for (start in starts) {
    end <- min(start + chunk_size - 1, n)
    chunk <- df[start:end, ]
    
    insert_sql <- DBI::sqlAppendTable(
      con_snl,
      table = DBI::Id(table = table_name),
      values = chunk,
      row.names = FALSE
    )
    
    DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, insert_sql)
    
    message("Uploaded rows ", start, " to ", end, " of ", n)
  }
  
  row_count <- DBI::dbGetQuery(
    con_snl,
    paste0("SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_rows FROM `", table_name, "`;")
  )
  
  message("Finished uploading `", table_name, "` with ", row_count$n_rows, " rows.")
  
  tibble::tibble(
    table_name = table_name,
    uploaded_rows = row_count$n_rows
  )
}


A few choices here are worth calling out because they generalize to any upload pipeline, not just this one:
The function drops and recreates the table before uploading, rather than appending to whatever might already be there. This makes the script idempotent: you can re-run it as many times as you want, and you’ll always end up with a clean, current copy of the data, rather than accumulating duplicate rows on every re-run.
The row-count confirmation at the end closes the loop. It is important to verify that the row count of the uploaded tables aligns with the known number of rows of the CSV file, from the earlier validation step.


Show the code
upload_results <- purrr::map_dfr(csv_files, upload_csv_to_sql)
upload_results


At this point, every CSV is now a SQL table, with column types automatically inferred. Typically types are assigned by SQL reading the first rows and observing each column, making an inference of type, which tends to be vague to avoid errors (ints can be classified as double floating point numbers but not the other way around necessarily).

title: SQL Upload Guide
section: Part 2: What Makes a Relational Database “Relational” — Keys
text:
Part 2: What Makes a Relational Database “Relational” — Keys
Loading data into tables gets you tables in a SQL server, but not yet a SQL relational database in the meaningful sense. What turns a set of unrelated tables into an actual relational database is the presence of keys: the formal mechanism that encodes how tables relate to each other and what counts as a valid row.
This is the part of the process most worth understanding deeply, because the concepts here — primary keys, foreign keys, referential integrity — apply identically whether you’re working with SNL sketches, clinical trial data, survey panels, or financial transactions.

Two Types of Keys in SQL
A primary key is a column (or combination of columns) that uniquely identifies every row in a single table, with no duplicates and no missing values allowed.
A foreign key is a column in one table that refers to the primary key of another table. This is what makes joins between tables meaningful rather than coincidental.
For more information of keys along with indexing in SQL see the Informational Resource on Relational aspect of SQL and Keys.


Inspecting the Database Before Changing Anything
Before assigning any keys, the first move with any unfamiliar (or freshly uploaded) database is to look at what’s actually there.


Show the code
DBI::dbListTables(con_snl)


 [1] "actors"      "appearances" "casts"       "characters"  "episodes"   
 [6] "hosts"       "impressions" "seasons"     "sketches"    "tenure"     
[11] "titles"     




Show the code
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE actors;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE appearances;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE casts;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE characters;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE episodes;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE hosts;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE impressions;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE seasons;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE sketches;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE tenure;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE titles;")


DESCRIBE shows you each table’s columns, their current data types, whether they accept missing values, and any keys already defined. DESCRIBE is the equivalent of running str() or glimpse() on a data frame in R. This is the first thing you should do before drawing any conclusions about the data’s structure. Consider the following code tha describes (pun intended) the actors table:

DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE actors;")

   Field         Type Null Key Default Extra
1    aid varchar(255)   NO PRI    <NA>      
2    url         text  YES        <NA>      
3   type         text  YES        <NA>      
4 gender         text  YES        <NA>      




The Three Requirements for a Valid Primary Key
For a column to serve as a primary key, it must satisfy two conditions and ideally should meet an additional third condition:

No missing values. A key that’s sometimes absent can’t reliably identify every row.
Every value is unique. If a value repeats, it can’t distinguish between the rows that share it.
It represents a natural identifier for the entity. This one is more judgment-based. Choosing a column that means something as an identifier (an actor’s ID, a patient’s ID, a transaction’s ID) rather than an arbitrary column that happens to be unique by coincidence.

For the SNL data, the natural candidates were the existing ID columns scraped along with the data: actors.aid, characters.charid, episodes.epid, impressions.impid, seasons.sid, sketches.skid, and titles.tid1. This is a common situation where the data often arrives with implicit identifiers already in place, and using them as keys is best practice.


Checking Candidates Before Committing
Rather than trust that an ID column satisfies the three conditions above, it’s worth checking directly. This test of comparing total rows, non-missing values, and distinct values is a general-purpose validity check you can reuse for any candidate key, in any dataset:


Show the code
check_key <- function(table, col) {
  DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, paste0("
    SELECT 
      '", table, "' AS table_name,
      '", col, "' AS column_name,
      COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
      COUNT(`", col, "`) AS non_missing,
      COUNT(DISTINCT `", col, "`) AS distinct_values,
      SUM(`", col, "` IS NULL) AS missing_values
    FROM `", table, "`;
  "))
}

primary_key_checks <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  check_key("actors", "aid"),
  check_key("characters", "charid"),
  check_key("episodes", "epid"),
  check_key("impressions", "impid"),
  check_key("seasons", "sid"),
  check_key("sketches", "skid"),
  check_key("titles", "tid")
)

primary_key_checks


If total_rows, non_missing, and distinct_values are all equal, and missing_values is zero, the column passes the test. If any of those don’t line up, that’s valuable information for the user to have before committing to a key. That is, any column that fails the test should not become a primary key.


Identifying Foreign Key Relationships
Once primary keys are established, the next question is: which columns in other tables refer back to a primary key? This is fundamentally a modeling question about your data’s structure, and answering it well requires understanding what each table actually represents. Oftentimes, you can identify columns that have the same name across tables.
For the SNL database, the following connections were made: appearances links to actors, titles, characters, impressions, episodes, and seasons (an appearance is the intersection of all of these: a specific actor performing in a specific sketch, in a specific episode); casts links actors to seasons; episodes belongs to a season; hosts links hosts to episodes; titles links to episodes, seasons, and sketches; and tenure links back to actors.


Checking for Orphans Before Enforcing Anything
A foreign key constraint, once created, requires that every value in the child column exists in the parent table. Parent table contains the primary key and child column connects to that primary key. But real-world data is messy, and it’s common to have values that don’t satisfy the parent-child connection, called orphan values. SQL won’t let you create the foreign key constraint until the orphan values are resolved, so it is necessary to find them before trying to make a foreign key that won’t be accepted.


Show the code
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT c.*
  FROM casts c
  LEFT JOIN episodes e
    ON c.first_epid = e.epid
  WHERE c.first_epid IS NOT NULL
    AND e.epid IS NULL;
")

DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT h.*
  FROM hosts h
  LEFT JOIN actors a
    ON h.aid = a.aid
  WHERE h.aid IS NOT NULL
    AND a.aid IS NULL;
")


This LEFT JOIN ... WHERE parent.key IS NULL pattern directly answers “which rows in my child table don’t have a matching parent?” regardless of what the entities actually are.
In the SNL data, resolving orphan values surfaced two genuine problems: some casts.first_epid values didn’t match any real episode, and one hosts.aid value didn’t match any real actor. Scraped and merged data routinely has small inconsistencies like this, and finding them is the entire point of this check.


Deciding How to Handle Orphans
Once you’ve found orphan values, you have to decide what to do about them. Generally, the options are: correct the value if you can determine what it should have been, delete the row if it’s clearly invalid, or set the offending value to NULL if you can’t confidently do either of the first two options.


Show the code
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  UPDATE casts c
  LEFT JOIN episodes e
    ON c.first_epid = e.epid
  SET c.first_epid = NULL
  WHERE c.first_epid IS NOT NULL
    AND e.epid IS NULL;
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  UPDATE hosts h
  LEFT JOIN actors a
    ON h.aid = a.aid
  SET h.aid = NULL
  WHERE h.aid IS NOT NULL
    AND a.aid IS NULL;
")


Setting the observed value to NULL here is a deliberately conservative choice: rather than guessing at what the “correct” episode or actor should have been (and risking a false relationship that looks valid but isn’t), the missing-link information is preserved as openly missing.


Verifying the Fix
The same orphan-check pattern from before doubles as a verification step after the fix.


Show the code
check_fk <- function(child_table, child_col, parent_table, parent_col) {
  DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, paste0("
    SELECT 
      '", child_table, ".", child_col, " -> ", parent_table, ".", parent_col, "' AS relationship,
      COUNT(*) AS orphan_count
    FROM `", child_table, "` c
    LEFT JOIN `", parent_table, "` p
      ON c.`", child_col, "` = p.`", parent_col, "`
    WHERE c.`", child_col, "` IS NOT NULL
      AND p.`", parent_col, "` IS NULL;
  "))
}

fk_checks <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  check_fk("appearances", "aid", "actors", "aid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "tid", "titles", "tid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "charid", "characters", "charid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "impid", "impressions", "impid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "sid", "seasons", "sid"),
  
  check_fk("casts", "aid", "actors", "aid"),
  check_fk("casts", "sid", "seasons", "sid"),
  check_fk("casts", "first_epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  check_fk("casts", "last_epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  
  check_fk("episodes", "sid", "seasons", "sid"),
  
  check_fk("hosts", "epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  check_fk("hosts", "aid", "actors", "aid"),
  
  check_fk("titles", "epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  check_fk("titles", "skid", "sketches", "skid"),
  check_fk("titles", "sid", "seasons", "sid"),
  
  check_fk("tenure", "aid", "actors", "aid")
)

fk_checks


The test is applied across every foreign key candidate relationship, now we can see if each foreign key relationship is valid.


A Required Technical Prerequisite: Storage Engine and Data Types
Before constraints or foreign keys can actually be created, two technical prerequisites in MySQL/MariaDB specifically need attention.
Storage engine. Foreign keys require a storage engine that supports them: InnoDB, in MySQL/MariaDB’s case. Tables created through bulk CSV import sometimes default to a different engine, so the key storage needs to be checked and corrected:


Show the code
tables <- DBI::dbListTables(con_snl)

for (table in tables) {
  DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, paste0("ALTER TABLE `", table, "` ENGINE = InnoDB;"))
}


Matching data types. A foreign key constraint requires the child column and the parent column to have compatible types. Since automatic type inference during upload is often loose the column type needs to be specified (e.g., modifying DOUBLE to INT).


Show the code
# Primary key columns
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE actors MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE characters MODIFY charid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE episodes MODIFY epid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE impressions MODIFY impid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE seasons MODIFY sid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE sketches MODIFY skid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles MODIFY tid INT NOT NULL;")




Show the code
# appearances
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY tid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY charid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY impid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY epid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY sid INT;")

# casts
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts MODIFY sid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts MODIFY first_epid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts MODIFY last_epid INT;")

# episodes
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE episodes MODIFY sid INT;")

# hosts
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE hosts MODIFY epid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE hosts MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255);")

# titles
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles MODIFY epid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles MODIFY skid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles MODIFY sid INT;")

# tenure
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE tenure MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255);")


Notice that actors.aid is set to be a VARCHAR, not an integer — in the original scraped data, actor IDs are text-based codes rather than plain numbers. Every column referencing it (appearances.aid, casts.aid, hosts.aid, tenure.aid) must match that type exactly. This is a generalizable rule: a foreign key’s type is dictated by whatever the primary key it references actually is.


Creating the Primary Keys
With types corrected and orphans resolved, creating the primary keys themselves is mechanically simple:


Show the code
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE actors ADD PRIMARY KEY (aid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE characters ADD PRIMARY KEY (charid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE episodes ADD PRIMARY KEY (epid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE impressions ADD PRIMARY KEY (impid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE seasons ADD PRIMARY KEY (sid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE sketches ADD PRIMARY KEY (skid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles ADD PRIMARY KEY (tid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts ADD PRIMARY KEY (aid, sid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE tenure ADD PRIMARY KEY (aid);")


Two of the primary key creations in the SNL database deserve a second look, because they illustrate situations that come up regularly in real data modeling.
Composite keys. casts uses (aid, sid) — actor ID and season ID together — as its primary key, rather than any single column. This makes sense once you think about what a row in casts represents: one actor’s membership in one season. Neither aid nor sid alone is unique (an actor appears across many seasons; a season has many actors), but the combination is.
No natural unique identifier at all. Some tables don’t have any column or combination of existing columns that’s guaranteed unique or a meaningful identifier. appearances, in this case, does not have a unique identifier since a single actor could, in principle, appear multiple times in the same sketch in the same episode, with no existing column distinguishing those occurrences. The solution is a surrogate key: a new column, with no real-world meaning, that exists purely to give every row a unique handle.


Show the code
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD COLUMN appearance_id INT NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY FIRST;
")


AUTO_INCREMENT means the database assigns each new row the next sequential integer automatically. Surrogate keys are extremely common in practice; in fact, in many production database designs, every table gets an auto-incrementing surrogate key by default, specifically to sidestep the exact problem of no natural identifier before it ever comes up.


When the “Obvious” Key Doesn’t Quite Work
The original plan for hosts was a composite key of (epid, aid): the same logic as casts. But a primary key, by definition, cannot contain missing values in any of its component columns. So before committing to that plan, it’s worth checking:


Show the code
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT
    SUM(epid IS NULL) AS missing_epid,
    SUM(aid IS NULL) AS missing_aid
  FROM hosts;
")




Show the code
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT *
  FROM hosts
  WHERE aid IS NULL;
")


This check found exactly one row with a missing aid, likely a host who isn’t (or wasn’t, at the time of scraping) a cast member with an actor ID on file. Because of that single missing value, (epid, aid) is disqualified as a primary key outright; SQL would refuse to create it. The fix is the same surrogate-key pattern as before:


Show the code
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE hosts
  ADD COLUMN host_id INT NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY FIRST;
")


A key design that looks correct in theory can be invalidated by a single bad value in practice, and it’s much better to discover that during a deliberate check than to discover it as a cryptic constraint-creation error later. Always verify potential keys before you commit to establishing the keys associated with the database.


Creating the Foreign Keys
With primary keys, type compatibility, and orphan values all resolved, the foreign key constraints themselves can finally be added:


Show the code
# appearances foreign keys
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_actor
  FOREIGN KEY (aid) REFERENCES actors(aid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_title
  FOREIGN KEY (tid) REFERENCES titles(tid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_character
  FOREIGN KEY (charid) REFERENCES characters(charid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_impression
  FOREIGN KEY (impid) REFERENCES impressions(impid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_season
  FOREIGN KEY (sid) REFERENCES seasons(sid);
")

# casts foreign keys
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE casts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_casts_actor
  FOREIGN KEY (aid) REFERENCES actors(aid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE casts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_casts_season
  FOREIGN KEY (sid) REFERENCES seasons(sid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE casts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_casts_first_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (first_epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE casts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_casts_last_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (last_epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

# episodes foreign key
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE episodes
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_episodes_season
  FOREIGN KEY (sid) REFERENCES seasons(sid);
")

# hosts foreign keys
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE hosts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_hosts_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE hosts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_hosts_actor
  FOREIGN KEY (aid) REFERENCES actors(aid);
")

# titles foreign keys
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE titles
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_titles_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE titles
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_titles_sketch
  FOREIGN KEY (skid) REFERENCES sketches(skid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE titles
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_titles_season
  FOREIGN KEY (sid) REFERENCES seasons(sid);
")

# tenure foreign key
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE tenure
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_tenure_actor
  FOREIGN KEY (aid) REFERENCES actors(aid);
")


After keys have been established is the moment the database actually becomes “relational” in the formal sense. From this point forward, the database itself, enforces the relationships


Confirming the Final Structure
Finally, it’s worth confirming what was actually built, both as a sanity check and as documentation you (or a collaborator) can return to later.


Show the code
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT 
    table_name,
    column_name,
    constraint_name,
    referenced_table_name,
    referenced_column_name
  FROM information_schema.key_column_usage
  WHERE table_schema = 'snl'
  ORDER BY table_name, constraint_name, ordinal_position;
")




Show the code
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT 
    table_name,
    GROUP_CONCAT(column_name ORDER BY ordinal_position SEPARATOR ', ') AS primary_key
  FROM information_schema.key_column_usage
  WHERE table_schema = 'snl'
    AND constraint_name = 'PRIMARY'
  GROUP BY table_name
  ORDER BY table_name;
")


Querying information_schema on every standard SQL database shows its own structure through this same metadata schema, so the exact query pattern (perhaps with a different table_schemaname) works for inspecting keys in any database you encounter, not just this one.

title: Join Algorithms and Join Order in PostgreSQL
text:
A note on cold cache
Every measurement above is warm-cache: the warm-up run loads the relevant pages into PostgreSQL’s buffer pool, so the timed runs reflect the cost of the join algorithm rather than the cost of fetching data from disk. This is the right thing to measure for a query that runs often enough to keep its pages resident in memory.
A cold-cache test — closer to a first-of-the-day query, or an analytics query over tables too large to stay in RAM — would be a useful counterpart, but it is genuinely hard to do faithfully here. A real cold measurement has to evict data from two separate layers: PostgreSQL’s own shared_buffers, and the operating system’s page cache underneath it. Neither is reachable from inside a database session. DISCARD ALL only resets session state (planner settings, prepared statements, temp tables) and leaves both caches fully populated; flushing the OS page cache requires root access we don’t have in this environment. Reconnecting on each iteration looks colder but measures essentially the same warm pages.
So rather than report numbers from a harness that can’t actually clear the cache, we’ll state the expectation instead: with a true cold cache the order penalty should widen, not shrink. A bad join order carries more rows through each step and therefore touches more pages; when those pages must be read from disk instead of memory, the extra I/O falls hardest on exactly the plan that was already doing the most work — the large-table-first Nested Loop. The warm-cache results below are thus a conservative picture of how much join order matters; on cold data the gap between good and bad order would only grow.

title: Join Algorithms and Join Order in PostgreSQL
text:
Practical Takeaways
First, join order matters most for Nested Loops: leading with your most-filtered tables keeps the outer row count — and therefore the probe count — small. Second, the planner’s choices flow directly from its row estimates, so accurate statistics (and thus low q-error) are what let it pick the right strategy and order on its own. Forcing these settings is useful for understanding the engine, but in practice the lesson is to keep statistics fresh and write selective filters, then trust the planner.
